Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I didn't have a strict schedule because I couldn't always attend classes, but I made the most of the time I had. I'd listen to recorded lectures and podcasts while doing other things around the house, and then I'd have these intense study sessions when I could really focus. It wasn't organized in a traditional way, but I knew what worked for me.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was going on in my life. Some weeks I could barely find time to study at all, and then I'd have to cram everything into a few days. Deadlines definitely pushed me to work harder, but I tried to at least listen to something course-related most days, even if it was just audio while I was commuting or doing chores.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check deadlines or download materials I could use offline later. Sometimes my peers would message me about discussions happening on the platform, and that would prompt me to check in. I wasn't someone who logged in daily just to browse around - I had specific purposes when I went on there.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me because I had limited time and internet access wasn't always reliable. What became routine was connecting with my study group through other apps and listening to downloaded content. The LMS was more of a starting point where I'd grab what I needed and then work with it elsewhere.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really valuable to me because I learn best by hearing different perspectives and explanations. When peers would break down concepts in their own words, it clicked for me in ways that textbooks didn't. I also appreciated any audio or video content I could download and listen to repeatedly - that matched how I learn best.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials and PDFs because they just didn't stick for me the same way. I'm an auditory learner, so sitting and reading dense text felt like a waste of my limited study time. I'd rather listen to a lecture three times than read a chapter once, so I prioritized accordingly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would actually help me understand the material better, not just check a box. I valued things that I could engage with in my own way - discussions where I could hear others' thoughts, or content I could listen to while multitasking. If something felt like busywork, I'd skip it and focus on what actually helped me learn.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford time-wise. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional stuff? I'd only do it if I genuinely thought it would help me or if my peers recommended it. Sometimes the optional discussions were actually more useful than required readings, so I learned to be selective rather than completionist.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lectures and listen to them on repeat, sometimes while walking or doing housework. I also relied heavily on my peer study group - we'd have voice calls where we'd talk through concepts together. My notes from those conversations were some of my best study materials, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were so many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS would show nothing. Like when I'd spend hours listening to a downloaded lecture, pausing to take notes, rewinding parts I didn't understand. Or when my study group would have long discussions about exam topics over the phone. That was real learning, but it was completely invisible online.
Q11: All the time, that was basically my main strategy. I'd log in once, download everything I could, and then work with those materials for days or weeks without going back online. My internet situation wasn't always stable, so I learned to grab content when I could and make it last. One video might get watched ten times offline after a single download.
Q12: There were definitely moments when I felt disconnected, especially when I'd miss several classes in a row and feel like I was falling behind. Sometimes the boredom would hit hard - like I understood the material but the format just wasn't engaging me. Having my peer support group helped pull me through those patches though.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor for me. I have a lot going on outside of school, and sometimes the LMS just had to take a backseat. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd still try to listen to audio content passively, but active engagement with the platform dropped. I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q14: Sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other problems in my life, and the content just wouldn't sink in. Those sessions felt wasted, so I learned to recognize when I wasn't in the right headspace and just step away instead of pretending to study.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was feeling bored with certain topics, and part was just being overwhelmed with everything else. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I could focus better, and sometimes that worked out fine, but other times I cut it too close to deadlines.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline coming and suddenly I'd be logging in, checking requirements, submitting work. Without that pressure, I might go days without touching the platform. It's not ideal, but the deadline-driven approach worked for my grades even if my attendance looked terrible.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going with my approach even though it looked unconventional. Knowing my exam scores and assignment grades were strong reassured me that my offline study methods were working. Negative feedback would make me adjust, but honestly most of my feedback was positive so I stuck with what I was doing.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that I could make it work for my situation, which I appreciated. Having recorded content available meant I wasn't completely lost when I missed classes. But the structure also assumed regular online participation, which wasn't realistic for me, so I had to create my own parallel structure that worked better.
Q19: The availability of downloadable content made engagement possible for me - without that, I would have really struggled. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or constant platform presence. Discussion forums were good because I could engage on my own schedule, but synchronous stuff was nearly impossible for my situation.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, trying to understand the requirements, or getting distracted while online. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Someone could be logging in repeatedly because they're confused, or they might be using the LMS as a starting point but doing actual work elsewhere like I do.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was minimal - I barely logged in - but I earned a distinction because my actual learning happened offline. I listened to lectures repeatedly, studied with peers outside the platform, and really engaged with the material in ways that just weren't tracked. Low clicks doesn't mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed something and for how long they had it open, but they can't show understanding or real engagement. They miss all the offline work, the peer discussions, the repeated listening to downloaded content. They might accurately show participation patterns but completely miss learning depth.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the ideas, not just clicking through content. For me, it's when I'm actively trying to understand something, asking questions, connecting concepts together. It's when I care about learning the material, not just passing. That engagement can happen anywhere - on a bus listening to a lecture, talking with friends about concepts, or lying in bed thinking through a problem.
Q24: The LMS data would completely miss how much I relied on my peer support network and how much learning happened in those conversations. It wouldn't show my audio-based study approach or how I'd listen to the same lecture five times until I really got it. It also wouldn't capture that despite feeling bored sometimes, I was genuinely committed to understanding the material and doing well.
